title: Interpreting Topographical Maps
highYieldFacts: Topographical maps show relief and cultural features at large scale (e.g. 1:50,000).
Survey of India (SOI) is India's national mapping agency.
RF 1:50,000 means 1 cm on the map represents 50,000 cm (500 m) on the ground.
Easting is read before northing in a grid reference.
Four-figure grid references identify a 1 km square.
Six-figure grid references locate a point within 100 m.
Contour lines join points of equal height above mean sea level.
Vertical Interval (VI) is the height difference between adjacent contours.
Close contour lines indicate a steep slope.
Widely spaced contours indicate a gentle slope.
Concentric closed contours indicate a hill or mound.
V-shaped contours pointing upstream indicate a valley.
Contours bend upstream along valleys and downstream along spurs.
A saddle or col appears as an hourglass-shaped contour between peaks.
Index contours are thicker and labelled with elevation.
Perennial rivers are shown with continuous blue lines.
Seasonal streams are shown with dashed blue lines.
Black symbols represent man-made (cultural) features.
Brown lines represent relief (contours).
Green shading commonly represents forest or vegetation.
Nucleated settlements appear as dense clusters of building symbols.
Linear settlements develop along roads, rivers, or coasts.
Dispersed settlements show isolated buildings in farmland.
Metalled roads are shown in red or black depending on importance.
Magnetic declination is the angle between true north and magnetic north.
Grid north is the direction of grid lines on the map.
Representative Fraction has no units—the ratio is dimensionless.
Larger-scale maps (smaller RF denominator) show more detail.
Plateaus show widely spaced, nearly straight contours over large areas.
Ridges show elongated contours bulging toward lower ground.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Topographical maps show both natural relief and human features of an area using conventional signs, contours, and a grid system.

**Must know**
• Prepared by Survey of India (SOI) at scales like 1:50,000 and 1:25,000
• Show hills, valleys, rivers, roads, settlements, and vegetation
• Essential for planning, defence, trekking, and land-use studies
• Brown = relief (contours); blue = water; black = cultural features; green = vegetation

Topographical maps are large-scale maps that represent the **shape of the land (relief)** together with **cultural features** such as villages, roads, and railways.

Unlike small-scale atlas maps, toposheets allow you to measure distances, read heights, and identify landforms accurately. Survey of India publishes them with a standard legend so that users anywhere in the country can interpret them uniformly.

**Key skills:** reading scale, finding grid references, tracing contour patterns, and linking symbols to real-world features.
notes.explanation:
Think of a toposheet as a '3D landscape flattened onto paper'—contours give height, colours give feature type, and the grid lets you locate any point precisely.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-02 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Keep a real SOI toposheet in class; students remember contour shapes better when they trace them with fingers.
notes.examTip: ICSE often asks you to identify landforms from contour patterns—practice V-shaped valleys and concentric circles for hills.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Scale expresses the ratio between map distance and ground distance; RF scale allows direct conversion using the map's stated unit.

**Must know**
• Representative Fraction (RF): 1:50,000 means 1 cm on map = 50,000 cm (500 m) on ground
• Statement scale: '2 cm to 1 km' is easy for quick mental conversion
• Measure curved routes along roads or rivers with thread/string, then scale off
• Larger denominator = smaller scale map (less detail per cm)

**RF scale** is written as 1:n where both sides use the same unit. For 1:50,000, multiply map cm by 50,000 to get ground cm (÷100,000 for km).

**Worked example:** 3.2 cm on a 1:25,000 map → 3.2 × 25,000 = 80,000 cm = 800 m.

Always check whether the question gives km, m, or cm—unit errors are the commonest mistake in map-scale sums.
notes.explanation:
Scale is the bridge between the map and the real world; every contour height and grid distance calculation depends on it.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-01 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch1-03 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Have students convert 1:50,000 and 1:25,000 for the same line— they see why larger-scale maps show more detail.
notes.examTip: Show all working: map distance × denominator → convert cm to m/km in one clear step.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** The grid on SOI maps uses eastings (vertical lines, read first) and northings (horizontal lines) to give four-figure and six-figure references.

**Must know**
• Four-figure grid reference locates a 1 km square (e.g. 4523)
• Read easting first (45), then northing (23)—'along the corridor, up the stairs'
• Six-figure reference pinpoints 100 m within the square (e.g. 458237)
• Grid north may differ slightly from true/magnetic north—check map margin

To find a **four-figure reference** for a feature:
1. Identify the grid square it lies in.
2. Read the **easting** of the left grid line (first two digits).
3. Read the **northing** of the bottom grid line (last two digits).

For **six-figure** references, estimate tenths within the square: divide the square mentally into 10 parts along easting and northing.
notes.explanation:
Grid references are like addresses on the map—eastings increase eastward, northings increase northward.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-02 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch1-04 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Mnemonic: 'Easting before Northing' — same order as (x, y) in mathematics.
notes.examTip: Four-figure = square; six-figure = precise point. State which type the question demands.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Contours are imaginary lines joining points of equal height; their spacing reveals slope steepness and landform type.

**Must know**
• Vertical Interval (VI): height difference between successive contours (given in map margin)
• Index contours are thicker and labelled with height
• Close contours = steep slope; wide spacing = gentle slope
• Contours never cross each other (except overhanging cliff—rare at this level)

Each brown contour line is a slice through the landscape at a fixed height. If VI = 20 m, adjacent contours differ by 20 m in elevation.

**Reading slope:** Measure horizontal distance between contours using scale—fewer metres between contours means steeper gradient.

**Hilltop:** Outermost closed loop is lowest; inner loops rise to the summit where contours may form small circles.
notes.explanation:
Contours turn 3D relief into 2D patterns—learn the 'shapes' (circles, V's, U's) and you can 'see' the terrain.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-03 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch1-05 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Stack cardboard cut-outs at 20 m intervals to build a physical contour model.
notes.examTip: Always note the VI from the map key before calculating relative height between two points.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Hills, valleys, ridges, spurs, cols, and plateaus each produce distinctive contour arrangements.

**Must know**
• Concentric closed contours → hill or mound
• V-shaped contours pointing uphill → valley (river in the V)
• U-shaped wide spacing → broad valley or plateau edge
• Saddle/col: hourglass shape between two peaks
• Uniform spacing on a line → ridge or escarpment

| Pattern | Landform |
|---------|----------|
| Closed concentric loops | Hill / Dome |
| V contours upstream | Valley |
| V pointing downhill | Spur |
| Elongated parallel contours | Ridge |
| Very wide flat contours | Plateau / Plain |

Rivers cut valleys so contours bend **upstream** into the valley. Spurs are ridges between valleys; their V points **downhill**.
notes.explanation:
Contour vocabulary is the heart of topo interpretation—link each pattern to water flow direction.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-04 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch1-06 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Draw a river line and ask students to sketch expected contour bends before revealing the map.
notes.examTip: When identifying valleys, confirm with blue water symbols or drainage arrows if shown.

notes.subtopic: 6. Settlements and Land Use on Maps
notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Settlement patterns (nucleated, dispersed, linear) appear through building symbols and their relation to relief and transport.

**Must know**
• Nucleated: cluster of building symbols at crossroads or near water
• Dispersed: isolated huts scattered in agricultural areas
• Linear: buildings strung along a road, canal, or valley floor
• Dense black blocks may indicate urban areas or large villages

Settlements choose sites for **water, defence, transport, and fertile land**. On toposheets you infer:

- **Valley-floor villages** near perennial streams (perennial = solid blue line).
- **Linear settlements** along highways or railways (black/red lines).
- **Hill-top forts** or temples on concentric contours with track access.

Land use: yellow wash often shows cultivated land; green shading = forest or orchard (check legend).
notes.explanation:
Human geography on toposheets is about reading symbols in their physical context—not memorising symbols in isolation.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-05 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch1-07 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Compare two settlement types on the same sheet and list three map clues for each.
notes.examTip: Justify settlement type with at least two map evidences: contour, water, transport, or symbol density.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Rivers, streams, tanks, and wells use blue symbols; drainage pattern reflects underlying geology and slope.

**Must know**
• Perennial rivers: solid blue lines; seasonal: dashed blue
• Tributaries join main rivers; contour V's point upstream
• Tanks/reservoirs shown with embankment symbol and water fill
• Dendritic, trellis, radial patterns indicate terrain structure

**Perennial** water flows year-round (important for settlement). **Seasonal** streams appear only in monsoon—dashed lines warn of dry beds.

Watershed divides separate drainage basins—often along ridges where contours form elongated highs. River gradient can be inferred from how quickly contours cross the blue line.
notes.explanation:
Water and contours together tell the drainage story; always trace a river from source to mouth when analysing a sheet.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-06 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch1-08 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Colour perennial vs seasonal streams on a tracing—students grasp seasonality quickly.
notes.examTip: Mention perennial vs seasonal when explaining water supply for a mapped settlement.

notes.subtopic: 8. Conventional Signs and Map Marginal Information
notes.content:
**Executive summary:** SOI conventional signs standardise symbols for cultural and physical features; marginal information gives map metadata.

**Must know**
• Black: cultural (roads, buildings, names)
• Brown: relief contours
• Blue: hydrography
• Green: vegetation
• Red: major roads, grid numbers, sheet edges
• Margin shows scale, VI, magnetic declination, edition date

The **legend (conventional signs)** is your dictionary. Common exam symbols: cart track, metalled road, railway, post office, temple, bridge, embankment, sand area, marsh.

**Marginal information** includes:
- Sheet number and name
- RF scale and contour interval
- Magnetic north and grid north
- Co-ordinates of corners

Always read the margin before interpreting the body of the map.
notes.explanation:
Conventional signs + margin data = full map literacy; exams often test symbol identification from the SOI key.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch1-07 — previous topic
notes.teacherTip: Run a 'symbol speed quiz' using the SOI legend page.
notes.examTip: If unsure of a symbol, describe its shape and context—partial credit often follows logical reasoning.

questions.question: Which organisation publishes standard topographical maps of India?
questions.answer: Survey of India (SOI) prepares and publishes official topographical maps used in schools and field studies.
questions.examTip: SOI toposheets are the ICSE standard.
questions.teacherTip: Contrast SOI with GSI—rocks vs maps.

questions.question: On an SOI map, brown colour generally represents:
questions.answer: Brown depicts relief through contour lines and occasional rocky outcrop symbols.
questions.examTip: Colour code: brown=relief, blue=water, green=vegetation, black=cultural.

questions.question: RF scale 1:25,000 means 1 cm on the map equals:
questions.answer: 1 cm × 25,000 = 25,000 cm = 250 m on the ground.
questions.examTip: Divide cm by 100,000 to get km quickly.

questions.question: Which grid reference is more precise?
questions.answer: Six-figure references narrow location to about 100 m within the 1 km square.

questions.question: In a four-figure grid reference, which is stated first?
questions.answer: Read easting (horizontal) first, then northing—'along the corridor, up the stairs'.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Contour lines never cross one another on a standard hillside.
2. The vertical interval is constant for a given map.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Both statements are correct—contours meet only in impossible overhangs (not expected at Class 8), and VI is fixed for each sheet.
questions.examTip: UPSC-style: both facts are standard topo rules.

questions.question: Closely spaced contour lines indicate:
questions.answer: Height changes rapidly over short horizontal distance → steep slope.
questions.teacherTip: Walk students through spacing vs slope.

questions.question: Widely spaced contour lines most likely indicate:
questions.answer: Little height change over distance → gentle terrain.

questions.question: Concentric closed contour lines typically represent:
questions.answer: Closed loops rising inward (or descending outward) mark isolated high ground.

questions.question: V-shaped contours pointing uphill indicate:
questions.answer: Valleys cut into slopes; contours bend upstream forming a V toward higher ground.
questions.examTip: River lies in the V of the valley.

questions.question: A spur is identified when contour V-shapes point:
questions.answer: Spurs are ridge extensions between valleys; Vs point downhill on spurs.
questions.examTip: Valley Vs uphill; spur Vs downhill.

questions.question: Vertical Interval (VI) is:
questions.answer: VI measures elevation change between adjacent contour lines (e.g. 20 m).

questions.question: Index contours are:
questions.answer: Index contours help read elevation quickly—they are bold and numbered.
questions.examTip: Use index contours to count height.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Perennial rivers have solid blue lines on SOI maps.
2. Seasonal streams use dashed blue lines.
3. Contours cross rivers at right angles always.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 are correct; contours bend along valleys rather than crossing rivers at right angles.

questions.question: Nucleated settlements appear on maps as:
questions.answer: Nucleated pattern = many buildings grouped at one site.
questions.examTip: Link pattern name to map appearance.

questions.question: Linear settlements commonly develop along:
questions.answer: Transport and water access produce elongated settlement strings.
questions.examTip: Name the line feature in answers.

questions.question: Dispersed rural settlements are characterised by:
questions.answer: Farmsteads spread across fields typify dispersed pattern.
questions.examTip: Common in fertile plains with farming.

questions.question: Which feature is shown in blue on topo maps?
questions.answer: Hydrographic features use blue.

questions.question: Black symbols on SOI maps generally denote:
questions.answer: Buildings, tracks, and many labels appear in black.

questions.question: Green shading on toposheets usually indicates:
questions.answer: Green represents wooded or vegetated areas per SOI convention.

questions.question: To measure distance along a curved road, the best method is:
questions.answer: Lay string along the curve, mark length, straighten against scale.

questions.question: 1:50,000 is _____ scale than 1:25,000.
questions.answer: Higher denominator → smaller scale → less ground detail per cm.

questions.question: Magnetic declination on a map margin shows:
options: Difference between true and magnetic north
questions.answer: Declination corrects compass bearings to map (true/grid) north.

questions.question: A saddle (col) between two hills shows contours shaped like:
questions.answer: Saddles narrow between two summits—hourglass contour pattern.

questions.question: Plateau areas typically show:
questions.answer: Elevated flat surfaces have contours far apart over large areas.

questions.question: Ridges appear on maps as contours that:
questions.answer: Ridge lines are elongated highs—contours bend around them.

questions.question: The direction of increasing easting values is:
questions.answer: Eastings increase eastward—toward the right on standard grid maps.

questions.question: Six-figure grid reference 438276: digits 82 indicate:
questions.answer: In 438276, 43/27 are the square; 8 is easting tenth, 2 is northing tenth within square (read order carefully per question).

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Grid north aligns with vertical grid lines.
2. True north points toward the geographic North Pole.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Both statements are correct—they differ slightly; maps show convergence/declination data.

questions.question: Seasonal streams are important because they:
options: May dry up in summer affecting water supply
questions.answer: Dashed blue lines warn that water may be unavailable in dry season.

questions.question: Embankments near tanks are shown to indicate:
questions.answer: Tank bunds impound water; common in peninsular plateau mapping.

questions.question: A cart track differs from a metalled road on maps by:
questions.answer: Road hierarchy uses different line styles—consult conventional signs.

questions.question: When contours form a 'U' shape wide open downstream, the feature is often:
questions.answer: Wide U contours suggest a broad valley floor.
questions.examTip: Compare narrow V vs broad U.

questions.question: Spot heights are shown by:
questions.answer: Spot heights give precise summit or point elevations.
questions.examTip: Use spot height when contours unclear.

questions.question: Settlement near confluence of rivers suggests access to:
questions.answer: River junctions offer water, transport, and fertile alluvium.

questions.question: Tracing a river upstream on a map, contours should:
questions.answer: Valley contours V toward higher ground upstream.

questions.question: Which instrument helps find bearings in the field?
questions.answer: Compass measures direction; adjust for declination on map.

questions.question: 1 cm on 1:50,000 map equals ground distance:

Consider the following statements:
1. Representative Fraction is unitless.
2. Statement scale may use different units.
3. Graphic scale cannot be used on topo maps.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 are correct; graphic (linear) scales ARE used on SOI sheets.
questions.examTip: All three scale types can appear.

questions.question: Dendritic drainage pattern resembles:
questions.answer: Random branching on uniform rock → dendritic (tree-like).

questions.question: Trellis drainage indicates:
options: Alternate hard and soft rock bands
questions.answer: Tributaries join main river at right angles on folded strata.

questions.question: Radial drainage flows:
options: Outward from a central high point
questions.answer: Volcanic cones or domes produce radial streams.

questions.question: Marsh or swamp may be shown by:
questions.answer: Poorly drained areas have specific wetland symbols.

questions.question: Bridges are important map features because they show:
questions.answer: Bridges link settlements and roads across drainage.

questions.question: A temple symbol near clustered buildings suggests:
questions.answer: Religious sites often anchor nucleated villages.

questions.question: Contour lines on a cliff may:
questions.answer: Very steep cliffs show contours nearly on top of each other.

questions.question: Yellow wash on SOI maps often indicates:
questions.answer: Agricultural land commonly appears with yellow tint (verify legend).

questions.question: Railway lines on toposheets are typically shown in:
options: Black with cross ticks or red per edition
questions.answer: Railways use distinctive line styles with ticks—check sheet legend.

questions.question: Height of a point between two contours can be estimated by:
questions.answer: If point lies midway between 100 m and 120 m contours, estimate ~110 m.

questions.question: Watershed is best described as:
questions.answer: Ridges often form watersheds separating river systems.

questions.question: Fort or defensive site on hills is suggested by:
options: Building symbols on concentric contours with steep approaches
questions.answer: Height + steep slopes favoured historic defence.

questions.question: Open scrub may be shown with:
options: Green dots or specific scrub symbol
questions.answer: Vegetation types have varied symbols—consult key.

questions.question: Map edition date in the margin indicates:
questions.answer: Currency of features depends on survey/revision date.
questions.examTip: Outdated maps may lack new roads.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Easting values increase eastward.
2. Northing values increase northward.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Both standard grid conventions are correct.

questions.question: To find area of a regular-shaped field on a map, you need:
options: Scale to convert map area to ground area
questions.answer: Area scales with the square of the linear scale factor.

questions.question: Gorge feature shows contours:
options: Very close together in narrow cut
questions.answer: Deep narrow valleys have tightly packed contours.

questions.question: Intervisibility between two points depends on:
options: Whether relief blocks the line of sight
questions.answer: A hill between points blocks view—use contour heights.

questions.question: Bench mark (BM) on survey maps indicates:
questions.answer: BM provides accurate height control for mapping.

questions.question: Levee along a river may appear as:
questions.answer: Natural levees from silt deposition along floodplains.

questions.question: Sand dunes in desert areas may show:
options: Special sand dune symbols or stipple
questions.answer: Arid region symbols differ—use legend.

questions.question: Canals appear as:
questions.answer: Irrigation canals are cultural hydrographic features.

questions.question: Quarry or mine symbols indicate:
questions.answer: Economic activity shown in cultural symbols.

questions.question: Contour numbering increases toward:
questions.answer: Numbers rise toward summits—verify with adjacent heights.

questions.question: A break in slope (knickpoint) may show as:
options: Change in map colour to green only
questions.answer: Tighter spacing after wide spacing marks steeper section.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Spot heights supplement contours on steep peaks.
2. Bench marks provide survey control.
3. Grid references use latitude and longitude.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 correct; grid refs use eastings/northings not lat/long on SOI topo sheets.

questions.question: The main purpose of topographical maps in planning is to:
options: Show accurate relief and land use for development decisions
questions.answer: Planners use relief, drainage, and settlements for roads, dams, and services.

questions.question: Identifying a water divide helps to:
questions.answer: Divides determine which river system receives runoff.

questions.question: Steep slope farming risk is inferred when:
options: Contours are very close on hillside fields
questions.answer: Close contours on slopes → erosion and landslide risk.

questions.question: Conventional signs are important because they:
questions.answer: Uniform legend enables anyone trained to read any SOI sheet.

questions.question: When drawing a cross-section between two points:
options: Plot heights from contour crossings in order
questions.answer: Mark where section line crosses contours; plot height vs distance.

questions.question: Topographical maps show only political boundaries.
questions.answer: False. They emphasise relief (contours), drainage, vegetation, transport, and settlements—not just boundaries.

questions.question: On SOI maps, easting is read before northing in a grid reference.
questions.answer: True. Standard order: easting first (horizontal), northing second (vertical).

questions.question: Closely spaced contours always indicate a gentle slope.
questions.answer: False. Close spacing means rapid height change → steep slope.

questions.question: Perennial rivers are shown with dashed blue lines.
questions.answer: False. Perennial rivers use continuous blue lines; dashed lines usually mean seasonal streams.

questions.question: Representative Fraction 1:50,000 means 1 unit on the map equals 50,000 of the same unit on the ground.
questions.answer: True. RF is a ratio without units—1 cm : 50,000 cm.

questions.question: V-shaped contours pointing uphill indicate a spur.
questions.answer: False. Vs pointing uphill indicate valleys; spurs show Vs pointing downhill.

questions.question: Six-figure grid references are more precise than four-figure references.
questions.answer: True. Six figures locate a point within about 100 m inside the grid square.

questions.question: Green colour on SOI toposheets commonly represents vegetation.
questions.answer: True. Green shading/symbols mark forest or vegetated areas per the legend.

questions.question: Contours can cross each other on a normal hillside.
questions.answer: False. Contours of different heights do not cross except in rare overhanging cliff representations.

questions.question: Linear settlements often develop along transport routes such as roads or railways.
questions.answer: True. Elongated building patterns follow linear infrastructure or valley floors.

questions.question: Contour lines join points of equal ______.
questions.answer: Height (elevation above mean sea level). Adjacent contours differ by the Vertical Interval.

questions.question: The height difference between successive contour lines is called the Vertical ______.
questions.answer: Vertical Interval (VI)—stated in the map margin (e.g. 20 m).

questions.question: In grid references, ______ is read before northing.
questions.answer: Easting (the horizontal coordinate) is always stated first.

questions.question: A RF of 1:50,000 means 1 cm on the map represents ______ cm on the ground.
questions.answer: 50,000 cm, which equals 500 m or 0.5 km.

questions.question: Man-made features on SOI maps are generally shown in ______ colour.
questions.answer: Black is used for most cultural features such as buildings, tracks, and labels.

questions.question: Match contour patterns with landforms:
questions.answer: Concentric→Hill; V uphill→Valley; wide flat→Plateau; hourglass→Saddle.

questions.question: Match colours with features:
questions.answer: Brown→relief; Blue→water; Green→vegetation; Black→cultural.

questions.question: Match settlement types with descriptions:
questions.answer: Nucleated→cluster; Dispersed→scattered; Linear→along line; Urban→dense block.

questions.question: Match scale with ground distance (1 cm):
questions.answer: Multiply cm by denominator; convert to m/km.

questions.question: Match drainage patterns:
questions.answer: Dendritic→tree; Trellis→right angles; Radial→centre; Parallel→subparallel.

questions.question: Match grid reference types:
questions.answer: 4523→square; 452387→point; 45→easting; 23→northing.

questions.question: Match water line types:
questions.answer: Solid→perennial; dashed→seasonal; tank→stored; well→groundwater.

questions.question: Match slope with contour spacing:
questions.answer: Steep→close; gentle→wide; cliff→touching; flat→very wide.

questions.question: Match map tools/methods:
questions.answer: Thread→curve; compass→bearing; RF→distance; section→profile.

questions.question: Match marginal info:
questions.answer: VI→height gap; declination→north angle; sheet→ID; edition→currency.

questions.question: Explain how contour lines show a valley on a topographical map.
questions.answer: Valleys appear as V-shaped contour bends pointing upstream (toward higher ground). A stream often occupies the valley floor shown in blue. The V points uphill because the land rises on both sides of the watercourse.

questions.question: Distinguish between four-figure and six-figure grid references.
questions.answer: Four-figure references (e.g. 4523) identify a 1 km grid square. Six-figure references (e.g. 452387) subdivide the square into tenths, locating a point within about 100 m. Both read easting before northing.

questions.question: Give three map evidences for a nucleated settlement.
questions.answer: Dense cluster of building symbols at one site; proximity to water (solid blue line) or road junction; possible temple/post office symbols indicating a service centre.

questions.question: How do you calculate ground distance using RF 1:50,000?
questions.answer: Measure map distance in cm, multiply by 50,000 to get ground cm, then convert: divide by 100 for metres or by 100,000 for kilometres. Example: 4 cm × 50,000 = 200,000 cm = 2 km.

questions.question: Why are conventional signs important on topographical maps?
questions.answer: They standardise symbols nationwide so users can interpret any SOI sheet consistently. Without them, relief, water, vegetation, and cultural features could not be read accurately for navigation and planning.
